data.title: Key deals this week: FuboTV - Disney, Constellation Energy, Eli Lilly, Phillips 66, Intercontinental Exchange and more
data.description:
The mergers and acquisitions market has already seen a flurry of deals at the start of 2025 across sectors. Here's a look at some of the key transactions:

Constellation Energy (NASDAQ:CEG) hit an all-time high yesterday after agreeing to buy privately-held natural gas and geothermal company Calpine for $26.6 billion.

Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) is in advanced discussions to purchase cancer-focused biotech Scorpion Therapeutics for as much as $2.5 billion.

data.title: Data center infrastructure revenue to reach $282B in 2024, capacity to triple by 2030
data.description:
Total data center infrastructure equipment revenues probably reached $282B in 2024, and hyperscale data center capacity is expected to triple by 2030, according to analytics firm Synergy Research Group.

The artificial intelligence (AI) boom has led to massive demand for high-powered graphics processing units ((GPUs)), which in turn has led to major investments in data centers to support those chips.

“GPUs and generative AI systems lit a fire under the market in 2024, resulting in record growth rates for the industry. While the ongoing success of public cloud has been the main driving force behind data center investments for well over a decade now, no-one imagined a 2024 market for data center gear reaching over $280B,” John Dinsdale, a chief analyst at Synergy Research Group, said on Tuesday.

See the following graphic shared by Synergy:

Overall worldwide spend on data center hardware and software in 2024 probably grew 34% Y/Y, Synergy said, primarily due to a 48% jump in spending on public cloud infrastructure.

Market Leaders of 2024

Synergy's figures are based on actual data for the first three quarters of 2024, along with the analytics firm's forecast for Q4.

Excluding original design manufacturers, Synergy found that Dell (DELL) was the overall leader in server and storage segment revenues. Meanwhile, Cisco (CSCO) emerged as the leader in the networking segment, while Microsoft (MSFT) featured prominently in the rankings "due to its position in server OS and virtualization applications."

"Nvidia (NVDA) now features heavily as a supplier both to other system vendors and directly to service providers. Outside of these five, the other leading vendors in the market are HPE (HPE), Super Micro (SMCI), Lenovo (OTCPK:LNVGY)(OTCPK:LNVGF), VMware (AVGO), Huawei, IBM (IBM) and Arista Networks (ANET)," Synergy said.

'Supercharged' Generative AI

Synergy Research believes that the average capacity of hyperscale data centers to be opened over the next four years will be almost double that of current capacity.

“The number of operational hyperscale data centers continues to grow inexorably, having doubled over the past five years. That installed base will continue to grow, but the most marked change in the market is the ever-increasing capacity of data centers that are being brought online," Synergy's John Dinsdale said on Thursday.

"The trend has always been for the critical IT load of hyperscale data centers to grow in size over time, but generative AI technology and services are power hungry and have supercharged that trend. Meanwhile, as the average IT load of individual data centers ramps up, the number of operational hyperscale data centers will continue to steadily grow," the analytics firm added.

See the following graphic shared by Synergy:

Here are some exchange-traded funds of interest tied to the technology sector, AI and robotics: (VGT), (NYSEARCA:XLK), (IYW), (FTEC), (IXN), (RSPT), (AIQ), (BOTZ), (DTEC), (GINN), (IRBO), (ROBT), (TECB), (XT), (THNQ), and (CHAT).

data.title: Bulls vs. Bears: Is Palantir stock a Sell as ARK Invest cuts stake?
data.description:
Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR), one of the best-performing tech stocks in 2024, fell roughly 13% over the past week, underperforming the NASDAQ Composite Index (COMP: IND), amid a ~$15M stake sale from ARK Invest.

After a series of similar sales over the past two months, does Cathie Wood-led investment firm’s latest decision to offload roughly 196.7K shares in Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) indicate a bearish outlook for the Denver, Colorado-based data analytics platform?

The Bulls

“Palantir's strong government and military contracts, particularly with the U.S. Army and AI initiatives, highlight its competitive advantages and growth prospects,” argued SA analyst Tangerine Tan Capital in “Palantir Stock: Remains Attractive For Those Looking To Invest For The Long Term.” “The biggest risk is share dilution; however, PLTR's unique position and exceptional AI capabilities make it a compelling long-term investment.”

“Despite a high forward P/E ratio, Palantir's exceptional EPS growth justifies its valuation, with potential for additional upside,” added SA analyst Noah's Arc Capital Management in “Palantir: Winning The Race To AGI.” “I think Palantir should be trading at an 800% premium to the sector median price-to-earnings ratio due to their 1125.08% projected premium to the sector median YoY EPS growth.”

The Bears

“Palantir's valuation is unsustainable, trading at extreme multiples, with a P/S ratio of 64.7x and forward P/E of 203x, far exceeding sector medians,” argued SA analyst Hataf Capital in “Palantir: Losing Momentum - Why 2025 Could Mark A Significant Downturn.” “Decelerating growth, massive insider selling, and extreme valuation metrics suggest a significant correction in 2025; I downgrade Palantir to Strong Sell.”

“Investors should be cautious as PLTR's valuation resembles overhyped COVID-era stocks,” added SA analyst The Software Side of Life in “Palantir: Applaud The Fundamentals, But Short Valuation.” “If the company were to guide 2025 revenue guidance similar to current estimates, the stock could potentially pull back significantly, as the valuation inherently implies upside to numbers.”

“Unfortunately, valuation analysis suggests that market participants have already fully priced in the potential of PLTR,” wrote SA analyst Selendis Research in “Palantir: Market Participants Have Already Fully Priced The Potential Of AI.” “Investors should avoid buying at the current price; those who already have positions should consider reducing.”

“If one ignores the valuation, there is indeed a lot to like about the stock. The company is GAAP profitable, has a net cash balance sheet, and revenue growth is accelerating,” opined SA Investing Group Leader Julian Lin in “Palantir: This Is Even Worse Than The 2021 Bubble.” “The stock's current valuation offers a nearly asymmetric return profile to the downside, warranting a “strong sell” rating,”

data.title: Iran selling oil stored in China to fund militias in Middle East: report
data.description:
Iran has shipped about 3M barrels from storage facilities in China to raise funds that may be spent on Tehran’s militia allies in the Middle East, The Wall Street Journal reported Saturday, citing people familiar with the matter.

According to the report, the oil is from stockpiles of at least 25M barrels Iran shipped to China in 2018, fearing new sanctions from the then-Trump administration, the people reportedly said.

The oil has been stored at ports in Dalian and Zhoushan, and two vessels recently set sail for the Dalian, the people were quoted as saying.

Beijing approved the shipments in December after discussions with Iranian officials in November and last month, the people reportedly said. The people reportedly added that it wasn’t the first time Tehran sought to drawn down the oil for sale, but it was the first time China gave its approval.

The Journal reported, one of the ships, the Madestar, departed Dalian earlier this month with 2M barrels of oil, and the second, the CH Billion, is thought to still be docked in Dalian and to be loaded with 700K barrels, the people said.

The people were quoted as saying Madestar ceased transmitting its location and intended route under the AIS international signal system earlier this month for three days while it was offshore Dalian and traveled to the port during that time. They reportedly said the ship headed to waters offshore South Korea to transfer oil to another vessel.

According to the people, CH Billion transmitted its position only briefly on Monday, the Journal reported.

Tehran could make up to $1B from the sale of the entire stockpiles, the people reportedly said, but it owes China about $1B in storage fees.

Some of the people were quoted as saying that Iran has earmarked the gains from selling the oil to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), which arms and funds Tehran’s militia allies around the Middle East.

One of the people said the U.S. has raised its concerns to China about funds going to the IRGC through its dealings with Iran.

data.title: Trending stocks amid jittery week for Wall Street
data.description:
The stock market started off the first proper week of 2025 with a stumble, as the S&P 500 index ended -1.3% end the end of the four trading days.

A benchmark index on Friday saw its biggest rout since December 18. Over the course of the week, market participants digested economic data on the labor market showing resilience as well as inflation data showing negative signals, including consumers inflation expectations hitting the highest level since 2008.

The Nasdaq Composite sank 1.65% while the Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped 1.3. The small-cap Russell 2000 index lost 2.22%. Wall Street’s volatility gauge, the VIX, jumped to around 20.

Amid a nervy week for the market, these were some of the trending stocks:

Constellation Energy (NASDAQ:CEG) hit an all-time high after agreeing to buy privately-held natural gas and geothermal company Calpine for $16.4B in a deal that will create the largest fleet of U.S. power stations. CEG closed +25.1% on Friday, reaching an all-time high of $310.

Quantum stocks tumbled after Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang said that “very useful” quantum computers are likely decades away. Quantum Computing (NASDAQ:QUBT) fell -50% in the week, D-Wave Quantum (NYSE:QBTS) erased -36.8%, Rigetti Computing (NASDAQ:RGTI) -52.6% and IonQ (NYSE:IONQ) plunged -25.5%.

Constellation Brands (NYSE:STZ) erased ~17% in the week after falling short of consensus estimates with its FQ3 earnings report. The management noted that the company continues to face subdued spend and value seeking behaviors that emerged among legal drinking age consumers in FQ2.

Delta Air Lines (NYSE:DAL) advanced 13.3% after topping profit estimates for the fourth quarter, led by growth in both the domestic and overseas markets.

Property & casualty insurance stocks fell as they're poised to see billions of dollars of claims from the Los Angeles wildfires that have scorched thousands of acres in one of the U.S.'s densest and most affluent regions. Mercury General (NYSE:MCY) plunged 25.7%, Allstate (NYSE:ALL) -6%, Travelers (NYSE:TRV) -3.8%, AIG (NYSE:AIG) -3.39%, Progressive (NYSE:PGR) -2.7%, and Hartford Financial Services (NYSE:HIG) declined 3.35%.

Walgreens Boots Alliance (NASDAQ:WBA) shares gained after the pharmacy chain reported upbeat fiscal Q1 results.

ON Semiconductor (NASDAQ:ON) dipped 12.9% after Truist Securities downgraded the shares to Hold from Buy.

Rigetti Computing (RGTI), down 52.6% in the week, Microvast Holdings (NASDAQ:MVST) -4.47%, Plug Power (NASDAQ:PLUG) +23.4%, Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR) -10.6% and Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA) +27.8% were among the most traded stocks in the U.S. market this week.

Mobileye (NASDAQ:MBLY) fell 22.3% as investors digested the company's appearance at the Consumer Electronics Show. Some analysts expressed concern that the autonomous driving assistance software company did not provide any commercial updates, after having done so at last year's event.

Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (NASDAQ:AMD) made headlines on Monday as each chipmaker unveiled their widely anticipated latest artificial intelligence-infused offerings at the Consumer Electronics Show. Nvidia introduced its newest AI-infused GeForce GPU, the GeForce RTX 50 series, which is part of the RTX Blackwell family. The GPU includes 1.8TB memory from Micron (NASDAQ:MU), which rallied 13% on the announcement.

data.title: Gilead in pact for anti-inflammatory drugs in a deal worth up to $1.7B
data.description:
Gilead Sciences (NASDAQ:GILD) and Danish pharmaceutical company LEO Pharma on Saturday announced an agreement worth up to $1.7B to jointly develop and commercialize LEO’s anti-inflammatory drugs.

As part of the deal, Gilead (NASDAQ:GILD) will buy LEO Pharma’s anti-inflammatory agents, which belong to a drug class known as STAT6 small molecule inhibitors.

The preclinical assets have already demonstrated potential against atopic dermatitis, asthma, COPD, and other inflammatory conditions, the companies said.

While GILD will lead their development as oral treatments, LEO will focus on their topical formulations.

In exchange, LEO is expected to receive up to $1.7B, including $250M as an upfront payment. It is also eligible for royalties ranging from high single digits to mid-teens based on sales of oral products.

Meanwhile, GILD is entitled to royalties ranging from high single digits to mid-teens on sales of topical products.

Foster City, California-based Gilead (GILD) projects the transaction to impact its GAAP and non-GAAP earnings per share by roughly $0.15-$0.17 this year.

data.title: Real estate stocks kick off 2025 on a negative note amid rising longer-term yields
data.description:
Real estate stocks kicked off 2025 on a negative note as rising longer-term Treasury yields continue to worry investors.

Concerns over the national debt and debt ceiling are at play, along with possible inflationary threats like higher tariffs, tax cuts, and mass deportations. Worries over fiscal discipline are also not restricted to the U.S., with borrowing costs in France now above those of Greece, while gilt yields in the U.K. have been pushed to their highest levels since 2008.

Investors are demanding larger premiums in response to the uncertainty.

The S&P 500 slipped 1.94% to end the week at 5,827.04 points. Among the sectors, Real Estate and Technology led the losers.

The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLRE) closed the holiday-shortened week 4.06% lower at 39.18.

Dow Jones REIT Indx Equity REIT Total Return Index (NASDAQ:REIT:IND) was down 4.22% from last week, while FTSE NAREIT Equity REITS (INDEXFTSE: FNER) fell 3.79%.

"A wider spread and a higher 10-year Treasury yield means higher mortgage rates. So maybe it's time to get re-used to these kinds of mortgage rates that were normal before 2008," said Seeking Alpha author Wolf Richter.

Company News

  • Camden Property Trust (CPT) has been added to the list of companies facing a lawsuit related to rental pricing. The U.S. Department of Justice said the defendants "participated in an unlawful scheme to decrease competition among landlords in apartment pricing, harming millions of American renters."
  • Crown Castle (CCI) CFO Daniel Schlanger will step down from the post, effective March 31.
  • American Tower (AMT) named Eugene "Bud" Noel, currently executive vice president and president of the U.S. Tower division, to the newly created global position of executive vice president and chief operating officer.
  • UBS Securities upgraded Digital Realty Trust (DLR) to Buy from Neutral as analyst John Hodulik believes "stronger renewal spreads, higher yields on new developments and a continuation of the bookings strength realized in '24" should bolster growth over the nearer-term.
  • BXP (BXP) announced the acquisition of 725 12th Street, a 300K sq. ft., 12-storey property in the East End of Washington, DC, for $34M.
  • Simon Property Group (SPG) owned department store chain, JCPenney, and Brookfield Asset Management has merged with SPARC Group to form a new company called Catalyst Brands.
  • The Federal Trade Commission has scheduled a closed-door meeting for next Thursday as FTC Chair Lina Khan's reign nears an end. Potential deals that the FTC may be looking at include CoStar's (CSGP) acquisition of Matterport.

Winners & Losers

This week, BXP (BXP) dragged the Office subsector down among the S&P 500 REIT subsectors. The stock lost ~9% of value. S&P 500 Office REITs closed the week 9.03% lower.

Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT) was the second major loser among individual stocks.

Welltower (WELL) and Ventas (VTR) were the top performers of the week, but were in the negative territory as well.

Residential REITs was the second major loser among REIT subsectors as mortgage rates continued to inch up.

"In the first full week of the new year, the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage remained elevated at just under 7 percent," Sam Khater, Freddie Mac's chief economist, said.

"The continued strength of the economy has put upward pressure on mortgage rates, and along with high home prices, continues to impact housing affordability. The lack of entry-level supply also remains an issue, especially for those looking to become first-time homeowners," Khater added.

Here is a look at the subsector performance for the week:

data.title: SEC Chair Gensler warns against weakening disclosure rules: report
C-3PO dreams: Tesla aims to sell humanoid robots to consumers in 2026
data.description:
Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) plans to begin selling Optimus robots to consumers in 2026 after producing several thousand this year. The humanoid robot has the potential to transform certain types of household assistance and industrial work.

The humanoid robot, standing at 5'8" tall and weighing about 125 pounds, is designed to perform various tasks in both industrial and domestic settings. Optimus is expected to be able to lift up to 150 pounds and carry 45 pounds while walking at 5 mph. In addition, Optimus is being designed to navigate uneven terrain and maintain balance using onboard sensors.

At CES, Elon Musk hyped the potential of the AI-trained robots. "I think most people, if not everyone, would probably like to have their own personal C-3PO or R2-D2," extolled Musk. "Humanoid robots [can] be the biggest product ever in history, by far," he added. His view is that humanoid robots will eventually outnumber humans by a large margin.

On the business side, Musk thinks humanoid robots will be priced at $20K to $25K each. He sees sales of the Optimus robot hitting 500,000 units by 2027.

On Wall Street, Deutsche Bank analyst Edison Yu estimated Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) might sell 200,00 humanoid robots a year by 2035. He valued the business at $22 a share, or about $70 billion. Bank of America's John Murphy and Wedbush Securities' Dan Ives also think Optimus development is poised to accelerate this year.

On Seeking Alpha, analyst Steven Fiorillo noted that Tesla (TSLA) is in a prime position to utilize its manufacturing expertise to produce Optimus humanoid robots at a scale that competitors are unlikely to match. Meanwhile, analyst Brett Ashcroft Green has a deep dive on how to construct an all-robotics portfolio.

Other companies working on humanoid robot development include Boston Dynamics, Agility Robots, Figure AI, Sanctuary AI, Apptronok, Engineered Arts, OpenAI, Hanson Robotics, UBTech Robotocs, and PAL Robotics. Companies testing industrial and manufacturing use cases for humanoid robots include Amazon (AMZN), BMW (BMWYY), Hyundai (OTCPK:HYMTF), and Mercedes-Benz (OTCPK:MBGAF). Some of those companies are also looking at household applications for a humanoid robot.

data.title: Nvidia at CES: Hit or Flop?
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) made headlines at the just-concluded Consumer Electronics Show (CES) in Las Vegas as its CEO and founder, Jensen Huang, delivered a keynote address and unveiled a slew of new products, kicking off the four-day event.

However, the chipmaker's stock sharply underperformed this week, even though the company showcased some of its most advanced AI innovations during the event, one of the most powerful tech gatherings in the world. While the NASDAQ Composite Index (COMP: IND) fell ~4% last week, Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) dropped ~9% following a massive rally that eclipsed its MAG 7 peers for much of 2024.

Did the company's CES performance disappoint investors or exceed their expectations?

According to Wedbush Securities, Nvidia (NVDA) used the event to extend its "enormous technology lead" in the semiconductor industry and over other tech rivals. "The overriding message from Jensen was a slew of new AI technology is coming out of Nvidia around robotics, autonomous technology, and PCs that will further stretch their enormous technology lead vs. the rest of the semi and Big Tech landscape," analyst Dan Ives wrote.

Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha Investing Group Leader James Foord upgraded the Santa Clara, California-based tech giant after the event, noting that "Nvidia Corporation's CES announcements, including new AI hardware and partnerships, signal a strong future in AI." "Despite the market sell-off, Nvidia's valuation remains attractive with significant growth potential, making it a compelling investment opportunity," Foord added.

According to Seeking Alpha analyst Bob O'Donnell, Nvidia's (NVDA) "most intriguing—but probably least understood"—announcement at CES was Cosmos, an AI-leveraged platform designed to accelerate the development of robotics and autonomous vehicles. Despite a potentially minimal short-term impact, in the longer term, "however, the impact could be profound, as it's expected to dramatically speed up the development of these product categories and improve the accuracy and safety of these applications," O'Donnell argued.

However, as the event came to a close, SA analyst Stuart Allsopp downgraded NVDA on Friday, noting, among other things, the profitability decline in its main customers, its rising account receivables, and Wall Street's peak optimism on the stock. "The failure of the stock to reach new highs recently, combined with recent bearish price action, suggests a potentially significant trend change could be afoot," Allsopp wrote.
